title_en: The Role of Hadith in Contemporary Islamic Law
title_id: Peran Hadis dalam Hukum Islam Kontemporer
content_en:
Al-Azami's fourth chapter bridges the historical and the contemporary, examining how the hadith corpus continues to function as a living source of Islamic legal guidance. Far from being a closed archive of ancient decisions, the hadith literature is the living foundation upon which Islamic jurisprudence operates in every generation, as each era's scholars apply the prophetic precedents to the novel situations their communities face.

The classical schools of Islamic law — Hanafi, Maliki, Shafi'i, and Hanbali — all depend on hadith as their second primary source after the Quran, though they differ in how they weight different categories of hadith and in how they reconcile apparent contradictions between narrations. The Maliki school gives particular weight to the practice of the people of Madinah, understanding that practice as itself a form of continuous Sunnah transmission. The Hanafi school applies sophisticated principles of hadith classification that led them to accept certain types of weak hadiths when supported by analogical reasoning or community practice. The Shafi'i and Hanbali schools are generally more willing to follow an individual authentic hadith even when it appears to conflict with analogical reasoning.

Contemporary issues — from bioethics to financial instruments to digital rights — require mujtahids (scholars qualified to derive rulings from primary sources) to search the hadith corpus for applicable prophetic precedents. A question about organ donation, for example, leads scholars to relevant hadiths about the sanctity of the body, the obligation to preserve life, and the concept of necessity (darura) in Islamic law. The hadiths do not provide a direct answer to every modern question, but they provide the principles and analogies that enable qualified scholars to derive well-grounded positions.

Al-Azami addresses the concern that Islamic law is static because it is based on ancient texts. This concern misunderstands the nature of the jurisprudential enterprise. The texts are fixed, but the methods of applying them are dynamic. The same hadith that governed commercial transactions in seventh-century Arabia can be applied to digital contracts in the twenty-first century through the principles of analogy (qiyas), consideration of public interest (maslaha), and customary practice ('urf) — all recognized tools of Islamic legal methodology. The stability of the textual foundation is a feature, not a bug; it ensures continuity and prevents the law from becoming a mere reflection of prevailing cultural fashions.

The chapter also examines the role of international fatwa bodies and contemporary scholarly institutions in applying hadith to collective issues. The Fiqh Council of the Muslim World League, the European Council for Fatwa and Research, and similar institutions bring together scholars from multiple schools to address issues requiring collective ijtihad. Their processes consistently begin with Quranic and hadith evidence before moving to analogical and rational tools, reflecting the enduring primacy of prophetic tradition in Islamic legal reasoning.

Al-Azami concludes that the relevance of hadith to Islamic law is not a historical accident but a structural necessity. A law that derives its authority from divine revelation must remain anchored to the forms in which that revelation was delivered and demonstrated. The hadith is not merely relevant today; it is indispensable.
content_id:
Bab keempat al-Azami menjembatani historis dan kontemporer, mengkaji bagaimana korpus hadis terus berfungsi sebagai sumber panduan hukum Islam yang hidup. Jauh dari menjadi arsip tertutup dari keputusan-keputusan kuno, literatur hadis adalah fondasi yang hidup tempat fikih Islam beroperasi di setiap generasi, karena ulama-ulama setiap era menerapkan preseden-preseden kenabian pada situasi-situasi baru yang dihadapi komunitas-komunitas mereka.

Mazhab-mazhab hukum Islam klasik, yakni Hanafi, Maliki, Syafi’i, dan Hanbali, semuanya bergantung pada hadis sebagai sumber primer kedua mereka setelah Al-Qur’an, meskipun mereka berbeda dalam cara menimbang kategori-kategori hadis yang berbeda dan cara mendamaikan pertentangan-pertentangan yang tampak antara riwayat-riwayat. Mazhab Maliki memberikan bobot khusus pada praktik penduduk Madinah, memahami praktik itu sendiri sebagai bentuk transmisi Sunnah yang berkesinambungan. Mazhab Hanafi menerapkan prinsip-prinsip klasifikasi hadis yang canggih yang membawa mereka untuk menerima jenis-jenis hadis lemah tertentu ketika didukung oleh penalaran analogis atau praktik komunitas. Mazhab Syafi’i dan Hanbali umumnya lebih bersedia mengikuti hadis sahih individual bahkan ketika tampak bertentangan dengan penalaran analogis.

Isu-isu kontemporer, dari bioetika hingga instrumen keuangan hingga hak-hak digital, mengharuskan para mujtahid (ulama yang berkualifikasi untuk menyimpulkan hukum dari sumber-sumber primer) untuk mencari dalam korpus hadis preseden-preseden kenabian yang dapat diterapkan. Pertanyaan tentang donasi organ, misalnya, membawa ulama-ulama ke hadis-hadis yang relevan tentang kesucian tubuh, kewajiban memelihara kehidupan, dan konsep darura dalam hukum Islam. Hadis-hadis tidak memberikan jawaban langsung atas setiap pertanyaan modern, tetapi mereka menyediakan prinsip-prinsip dan analogi-analogi yang memungkinkan ulama-ulama yang berkualifikasi untuk menyimpulkan posisi-posisi yang berpijak kokoh.

Al-Azami membahas kekhawatiran bahwa hukum Islam bersifat statis karena didasarkan pada teks-teks kuno. Kekhawatiran ini salah memahami sifat usaha yurisprudensial. Teks-teksnya tetap, tetapi metode-metode penerapannya bersifat dinamis. Hadis yang sama yang mengatur transaksi-transaksi komersial di Arabia abad ketujuh dapat diterapkan pada kontrak-kontrak digital di abad kedua puluh satu melalui prinsip-prinsip qiyas, pertimbangan kepentingan publik (maslahah), dan praktik adat (‘urf), semuanya merupakan instrumen yang diakui dalam metodologi hukum Islam. Kestabilan fondasi tekstual merupakan keunggulan, bukan kelemahan. Ia memastikan kesinambungan dan mencegah hukum menjadi sekadar cerminan dari mode budaya yang berlaku.

Bab ini juga mengkaji peran badan-badan fatwa internasional dan lembaga-lembaga ilmiah kontemporer dalam menerapkan hadis pada isu-isu kolektif. Majlis Fikih Liga Muslim Dunia, Dewan Eropa untuk Fatwa dan Penelitian, dan lembaga-lembaga serupa menyatukan ulama-ulama dari berbagai mazhab untuk menangani isu-isu yang memerlukan ijtihad kolektif. Proses-proses mereka secara konsisten dimulai dengan bukti Al-Qur’an dan hadis sebelum beralih ke instrumen-instrumen analogis dan rasional, mencerminkan primasi abadi tradisi kenabian dalam penalaran hukum Islam.

Al-Azami menyimpulkan bahwa relevansi hadis bagi hukum Islam bukan merupakan kecelakaan historis melainkan keharusan struktural. Hukum yang memperoleh otoritasnya dari wahyu ilahi harus tetap terikat pada bentuk-bentuk di mana wahyu itu disampaikan dan dicontohkan. Hadis bukan sekadar relevan hari ini. Ia mutlak diperlukan.
title_ar: دور الحديث في الفقه الإسلامي المعاصر
content_ar:
Al-Azami's fourth chapter bridges the historical and the contemporary, examining how the الحديث corpus continues to function as a living source of Islamic legal guidance. Far from being a closed archive of ancient decisions, the الحديث literature is the living foundation upon which Islamic jurisprudence operates in every generation, as each era's scholars apply the prophetic precedents to the novel situations their communities face.

The classical schools of Islamic law — Hanafi, Maliki, Shafi'i, and Hanbali — all depend on الحديث as their second primary source after the القرآن الكريم, though they differ in how they weight different categories of الحديث and in how they reconcile apparent contradictions between narrations. The Maliki school gives particular weight to the practice of the people of Madinah, understanding that practice as itself a form of continuous السنة النبوية transmission. The Hanafi school applies sophisticated principles of الحديث classification that led them to accept certain types of weak hadiths when supported by analogical reasoning or community practice. The Shafi'i and Hanbali schools are generally more willing to follow an individual authentic الحديث even when it appears to conflict with analogical reasoning.

Contemporary issues — from bioethics to financial instruments to digital rights — require mujtahids (scholars qualified to derive rulings from primary sources) to search the الحديث corpus for applicable prophetic precedents. A question about organ donation, for example, leads scholars to relevant hadiths about the sanctity of the body, the obligation to preserve life, and the concept of necessity (darura) in Islamic law. The hadiths do not provide a direct answer to every modern question, but they provide the principles and analogies that enable qualified scholars to derive well-grounded positions.

Al-Azami addresses the concern that Islamic law is static because it is based on ancient texts. This concern misunderstands the nature of the jurisprudential enterprise. The texts are fixed, but the methods of applying them are dynamic. The same الحديث that governed commercial transactions in seventh-century Arabia can be applied to digital contracts in the twenty-first century through the principles of analogy (qiyas), consideration of public interest (maslaha), and customary practice ('urf) — all recognized tools of Islamic legal methodology. The stability of the textual foundation is a feature, not a bug; it ensures continuity and prevents the law from becoming a mere reflection of prevailing cultural fashions.

The chapter also examines the role of international fatwa bodies and contemporary scholarly institutions in applying الحديث to collective issues. The الفقه Council of the Muslim World League, the European Council for Fatwa and Research, and similar institutions bring together scholars from multiple schools to address issues requiring collective ijtihad. Their processes consistently begin with Quranic and الحديث evidence before moving to analogical and rational tools, reflecting the enduring primacy of prophetic tradition in Islamic legal reasoning.

Al-Azami concludes that the relevance of الحديث to Islamic law is not a historical accident but a structural necessity. A law that derives its authority from divine revelation must remain anchored to the forms in which that revelation was delivered and demonstrated. The الحديث is not merely relevant today; it is indispensable.
